database_entries.reasoning: Direct match — total sales in Trading Account maps to CMA Domestic Sales (sheet_row 21, II_A1). No export sales for this trading firm. Sales breakdown by GST rate (18% IGST, 18% Local, 28% IGST, etc.) aggregated into single CMA row.
Component 1 of 4 for CMA Interest Received (sheet_row 29). Bank interest from savings accounts.

However, in FY2021-22, the SAME CA excluded PPF interest from II_B2 and placed it in II_B6a (Others Non-Operating Income). PPF is a personal/tax-exempt instrument. Treatment is inconsistent across years — likely a CA oversight or changed approach.
database_entries.reasoning: Component 2 of 4 for CMA Interest Received (sheet_row 29). PPF interest — exempt personal income but routed through business P&L in this proprietorship.
database_entries.validation_issue: Inconsistent treatment: FY22 excluded PPF interest from II_B2 (put in II_B6a), FY24 included it. Both approaches are defensible for a proprietorship.

database_entries.reasoning: Component 3 of 4 for CMA Interest Received (sheet_row 29). Fixed deposit interest.

database_entries.industry_note: In FY2021-22, the CA excluded PPF interest (Rs 24,577) from CMA Interest Received and only included business-related interest (Bank + FD = Rs 21,325). PPF interest was placed in II_B6a Others instead. This differs from FY2023-24 treatment where PPF interest WAS included in II_B2.

FY2021-22 specific mapping: Component 2 of 2 for CMA Interest Received. Sum: 4215 + 17110 = 21325 = CMA 0.21325 lakhs. VALIDATED ✓
Direct match — non-GST discount received maps to CMA Others Non-Operating Income (sheet_row 33, II_B6a). Small amount (Rs 213) in FY24; was Rs 3,364 in FY23 and Rs 23,941 in FY22.

database_entries.industry_note: FY2021-22 only: PPF interest placed in 'Others' non-operating income (II_B6a) instead of 'Interest Received' (II_B2). In FY2023-24, PPF interest was included in II_B2 instead. This reflects CA judgment on personal vs business income treatment in a proprietorship.
database_entries.reasoning: FY2021-22 specific: Component 1 of 2 for CMA Others Non-Op Income. PPF interest (24,577) + Discount Non-GST (23,941) + Round Off (-0.4) = 48,517.6 ≈ CMA 48,518.4 (minor rounding).
FY2021-22 specific: Component 2 of 2 for CMA Others Non-Op Income.

database_entries.industry_note: In trading companies, total purchases (cost of goods for resale) map to 'Raw Materials Consumed (Indigenous)' (II_C2). The CMA template is designed for manufacturing firms, so the CA uses the 'Raw Materials' row to capture trading purchases. This is standard practice for trading firms. Purchases include multiple GST-rate categories (18% IGST, 18% Local, 28% Local, 12% Interstate, 5% Interstate) netted with purchase returns and GST discounts.
database_entries.reasoning: Direct match — Total purchases (net of returns) in Trading Account maps to CMA Raw Materials Indigenous (sheet_row 41, II_C2). FY24: Rs 17,412,563.15 = 174.12563 lakhs. VALIDATED ✓
FY2021-22: Direct match — Transport Charges from Direct Expense schedule maps to CMA Freight (sheet_row 46, II_C7). In FY24, this row is zero; all direct expenses went to II_C9 Others.
database_entries.validation_issue: FY24: CMA Freight = 0 despite FS showing Transport Charges of Rs 171,318 in Direct Expenses. The CA moved all FY24 direct expenses to II_C9 Others instead of splitting transport to II_C7.

database_entries.industry_note: In this trading firm, electricity charges from the Admin Expenses schedule are placed in the Manufacturing section as 'Power, Coal, Fuel and Water' (II_C8). For a manufacturing firm, this would naturally be factory power. For a trading firm, the CA chose to put it in manufacturing costs rather than admin expenses. This is a CA judgment call — some CAs would put it in Admin 'Others' (II_D5) for a trading firm.
database_entries.reasoning: Direct match — Electricity Charges from Admin Expenses maps to CMA Power (sheet_row 47, II_C8). Note: the original spelling 'Electricty' (sic) is preserved from the financial statement.
Component 1 of 3 for CMA Others Manufacturing (sheet_row 48, II_C9) in FY24. All direct expenses placed here since II_C7 Freight = 0 in FY24.
Component 2 of 3 for CMA Others Manufacturing (sheet_row 48, II_C9).

database_entries.industry_note: In FY24, Transport Charges (Rs 171,318) was placed in II_C9 Others rather than II_C7 Freight. In FY22 and FY23, the same type of charge went to II_C7. The CA changed classification in FY24 — possibly because the amount grew significantly (from Rs 1,350 in FY22 to Rs 171,318 in FY24) and was reclassified or the CA used a different allocation approach.
database_entries.reasoning: Component 3 of 3 for CMA Others Manufacturing (sheet_row 48, II_C9). Sum: 550 + 3138.98 + 171318.13 = 175007.11 = CMA 1.75007 lakhs. VALIDATED ✓

database_entries.industry_note: In trading firms, opening stock (stock-in-trade) maps to 'Stock in process Opening Balance' (II_C12) rather than 'Finished Goods Opening Balance' (II_C17). The CMA template's manufacturing terminology treats trading inventory as WIP/stocks-in-process, not finished goods. This is standard CA practice for trading entities.
database_entries.reasoning: Direct match — Opening Stock in Trading Account maps to CMA Stock in process Opening Balance (sheet_row 52, II_C12).

database_entries.industry_note: Closing stock (stock-in-trade) maps to 'Stock in process Closing Balance' (II_C13a) for trading firms. Same logic as opening stock — trading inventory = WIP in CMA terminology.
database_entries.reasoning: Direct match — Closing Stock in Trading Account maps to CMA Stock in process Closing Balance (sheet_row 53, II_C13a).

database_entries.industry_note: ALL depreciation placed in Manufacturing section (II_C14), not Admin (II_D). For a pure trading firm with no factory/manufacturing activity, depreciation could logically go to Admin expenses. The CA chose the Manufacturing section — this is a judgment call. Assets being depreciated are cell phones, motor vehicle, air conditioner, television (all office/personal assets). Total depreciation is tiny (Rs 13,084 on 7 assets at 15% WDV).
database_entries.reasoning: Direct match — Total depreciation in P&L maps to CMA Manufacturing Depreciation (sheet_row 55, II_C14). FA schedule shows 7 assets (phones, car, AC, TV) all at 15% WDV rate.
Component 1 of 2 for CMA Salary and staff expenses (sheet_row 66, II_D1). FY24 saw a large increase from Rs 537,000 (FY23) to Rs 1,430,000.
Component 2 of 2 for CMA Salary (sheet_row 66, II_D1). Sum: 1430000 + 56331 = 1486331 = CMA 14.86331 lakhs. VALIDATED ✓
Direct match — Rent Account from Admin Expenses maps to CMA Rent, Rates and Taxes (sheet_row 67, II_D2). Consistent Rs 44,400 across FY23 and FY24.
database_entries.validation_issue: Professional tax (Rs 2,500) is NOT included in 'Rent, Rates and Taxes' — it goes to II_D5 Others. Only the rent component maps here.

database_entries.reasoning: Direct match — Bad Debts from Admin Expenses maps to CMA Bad Debts (sheet_row 68, II_D3). Only appears in FY24 (zero in FY22, FY23). First-time bad debt write-off for this firm.

database_entries.industry_note: IMPORTANT: 'Rebate & Discount' is classified under 'Advertisements and Sales Promotions' (II_D4) instead of being netted against sales. The CA classification rules (Rule 261) say trade discounts should be netted against Domestic/Export sales (II_A1). This CA chose to show it as a separate expense. This is a significant CA judgment call specific to trading firms with large rebate volumes (Rs 6.43 lakhs in FY24, Rs 10.64 lakhs in FY23). The classification choice affects Gross Sales and Cost of Sales ratios.
database_entries.reasoning: Direct match — Rebate & Discount maps to CMA Advertisements & Sales Promotions (sheet_row 69, II_D4). This is a CA judgment call that differs from standard classification rules.
database_entries.validation_issue: Contradicts CA classification rule 261 which says 'Trade Discount allowed' should be netted off against sales (II_A1). The CA chose to show it as a selling expense instead.

database_entries.reasoning: Component of CMA Others Admin (sheet_row 70, II_D5). Travel/transport expenses for business visits.
Component of CMA Others Admin (sheet_row 70, II_D5). Delivery/shipping costs charged to admin.
Component of CMA Others Admin (sheet_row 70, II_D5). Mobile phone expenses.
Component of CMA Others Admin (sheet_row 70, II_D5). Sales commission paid to agents/intermediaries.
database_entries.validation_issue: FY23: Commission on sales (Rs 3,500) may have been excluded from CMA Others, as the residual calculation shows a Rs 3,500 discrepancy.

database_entries.reasoning: Component of CMA Others Admin (sheet_row 70, II_D5). Professional consultancy charges.
Component of CMA Others Admin (sheet_row 70, II_D5). Sundry balances written off (negative = net recovery in FY24). Was positive Rs 1,275.94 in FY23.
Component of CMA Others Admin (sheet_row 70, II_D5). Freight charges from admin section (separate from Direct Expense transport charges which go to II_C9).
Component of CMA Others Admin (sheet_row 70, II_D5). Business insurance premium.
Component of CMA Others Admin (sheet_row 70, II_D5). State professional tax. Note: not grouped with Rent/Rates/Taxes (II_D2) — only Rent Account goes to II_D2 in this CA's classification.
database_entries.validation_issue: Professional tax could also map to 'Rent, Rates and Taxes' (II_D2) but the CA placed it in Others (II_D5), keeping II_D2 for rent only.

database_entries.reasoning: Component of CMA Others Admin (sheet_row 70, II_D5). General office expenses (stationery, supplies, etc.).
Component of CMA Others Admin (sheet_row 70, II_D5). Landline/telecom expenses.
Component of CMA Others Admin (sheet_row 70, II_D5). Business travel expenses. NEW in FY24 — not present in FY22 or FY23.

database_entries.reasoning: Direct match — Repair & Maintenace (sic — original spelling preserved) maps to CMA Repairs & Maintenance (sheet_row 71, II_D6). Zero in FY24. Note the misspelling 'Maintenace' in the original FS.

Direct match — Audit Fees from Admin Expenses maps to CMA Audit Fees (sheet_row 72, II_D7a). Called 'Auditing' (Rs 5,000) in FY22; renamed 'Audit Fees' (Rs 15,000) in FY24. Zero in FY23 (possibly bundled into Consultancy fees that year).

FY2021-22 variant: Same line item, different description — 'Auditing' (FY22) vs 'Audit Fees' (FY24). Both map to CMA Audit Fees (sheet_row 72, II_D7a). Capturing both phrasings for the training database.

database_entries.cma_field_name: Interest on Fixed Loans / Term loans
database_entries.industry_note: Interest Paid in Admin Expenses is mapped to 'Interest on Fixed Loans / Term loans' (II_F1), not to Admin section. This is interest on unsecured loans from parties (Jignesh C Mehta HUF, Newchem Pharma). Despite being 'unsecured' loans, the CA classified the interest under 'Fixed/Term Loans' interest. Note: the proprietor's housing loan interest (Rs 135,477 in FY24) is debited directly to Capital Account (personal expense), NOT in P&L.
database_entries.reasoning: Direct match — Interest Paid from Admin Expenses maps to CMA Interest on Term Loans (sheet_row 82, II_F1). Extracted from admin but classified as Finance Charges in CMA structure.
Direct match — Bank Charges from Admin Expenses maps to CMA Bank Charges (sheet_row 84, II_F3). Extracted from admin but classified as Finance Charges in CMA structure.
FY2021-22: Component 1 of 2 for CMA Bank Charges. FY22 also has 'Bill Discount Charges' (Rs 944.62) which likely also goes to II_F3.
FY2021-22 item present only in FY22 — bill discounting charges. May not be in II_F3 if CMA amount matches Bank Charges alone.
database_entries.validation_issue: Bank Charges (965.24) + Bill Discount Charges (944.62) = 1909.86 but CMA II_F3 FY22 = 965.24. The Bill Discount Charges may have gone elsewhere (possibly II_F1 interest or II_D5 Others). Reclassifying as likely II_D5 component instead.

database_entries.raw_text: Net Profit appropriated to Capital Account
database_entries.industry_note: In proprietorship firms, entire net profit is appropriated to the proprietor's Capital Account via 'Other Appropriation of profit' (II_I3). No retained earnings are carried to Balance Sheet (II_I4 = 0). This is because the proprietorship's capital account absorbs all profits and drawings. In a Pvt Ltd company, this row would show dividends or transfers to reserves instead.
database_entries.reasoning: Direct match — Full PAT appropriated to proprietor's capital. CMA II_I3 = PAT for all years. PBT = PAT since no tax provision at entity level (proprietorship).

database_entries.industry_note: Proprietor's Capital Account closing balance maps to 'Issued, Subscribed and Paid up' (III_L1) in CMA Form III. This is the proprietorship equivalent of share capital. The capital account includes: opening balance + net profit + personal income (gas subsidy, capital gains, dividends, IT refund) - drawings - personal expenses (income tax, housing loan interest, LIC, school fees, mediclaim, property tax, donations, share expenses).
database_entries.reasoning: Direct match — Proprietor's Capital Account closing balance maps to CMA Share Capital (sheet_row 115, III_L1). Capital Account is a running account that absorbs all profits and personal transactions.

database_entries.industry_note: Housing loan from Wankaner Society classified as 'Term Loan Balance Repayable after one year' (III_L4b). This is a PERSONAL housing loan of the proprietor — the entire balance is treated as long-term (>1 year) with zero current portion (III_L4a = 0). In a Pvt Ltd company, the current maturity portion would be shown in III_L4a. Declining balance: Rs 17.58L (FY22) → Rs 16L (FY23) → Rs 14.28L (FY24).
database_entries.reasoning: Direct match — Housing loan balance maps to CMA Term Loan >1yr (sheet_row 136, III_L4b).

database_entries.industry_note: Unsecured loans from Jignesh C Mehta (HUF) (Rs 268,860) and Newchem Pharma (Rs 1,011,726) classified as 'Quasi Equity' (III_L8a). Banks treat promoter/family unsecured loans as quasi-equity — subordinated to bank debt and treated similar to own funds for credit assessment. This is standard CMA classification for proprietor/family loans.
database_entries.reasoning: Direct match — Unsecured loans from parties maps to CMA Quasi Equity (sheet_row 151, III_L8a). Loans from promoter's HUF and associated firm treated as quasi-equity.

database_entries.industry_note: RECLASSIFICATION: In the FS, this property is under 'Investments (at cost)'. In the CMA, the CA moved it to 'Gross Block' (III_A1) under Fixed Assets. Banks prefer to see immovable property as fixed assets rather than investments. This is a proprietorship-specific CA judgment — the flat is a personal asset on the business BS, but classified as a fixed asset for bank CMA purposes.
database_entries.reasoning: Split from FS Investments → CMA Fixed Assets Gross Block. Flat 1 is 1/4th share of residential property. Together with Flat 2, these two properties account for Rs 22,11,013.50 of the CMA Gross Block.

database_entries.industry_note: Same as Flat 1 — reclassified from FS Investments to CMA Gross Block. Flat 2 is the larger property (Rs 18.99 lakhs vs Rs 3.12 lakhs for Flat 1).
database_entries.reasoning: Split from FS Investments → CMA Gross Block. CMA Gross Block = Flat 1 (311,788.50) + Flat 2 (1,899,225) + FS FA WDV opening (87,226.94) = 2,298,240.44 = 22.98 lakhs. VALIDATED ✓

database_entries.industry_note: CMA 'Gross Block' uses WDV OPENING balance (not historical cost) for depreciable business assets. Combined with the two flats (from Investments), this gives the total CMA Gross Block. The CMA 'Accumulated Depreciation' (III_A2) contains only CURRENT YEAR depreciation, not cumulative. This is a non-standard but common CA practice for proprietorship CMA forms.
database_entries.reasoning: Component of CMA Gross Block — the actual business fixed assets (phones, car, AC, TV) at WDV opening.
database_entries.validation_issue: CMA Gross Block = WDV opening (not original cost). CMA Accumulated Dep = current year dep (not cumulative). This means Net Block = WDV closing + Flats, which works mathematically but is semantically non-standard.

database_entries.industry_note: CMA 'Accumulated Depreciation' contains only the CURRENT YEAR's depreciation (Rs 13,084), not the cumulative total. This is non-standard — normally this field should show total accumulated depreciation since inception. The CA likely adopted this approach because the Gross Block is also non-standard (using WDV opening instead of historical cost).
database_entries.reasoning: Direct match — Current year depreciation used as CMA Accumulated Depreciation (sheet_row 162, III_A2).
database_entries.validation_issue: Contains current year depreciation only, not cumulative. This is consistent with the Gross Block also using WDV opening.

database_entries.industry_note: LIC Market Plus policy classified as 'Investment in Govt Securities (Current)' because LIC (Life Insurance Corporation) is a government entity. The CA treated this life insurance investment product as a government security. This is a common CA practice for LIC policies in CMA forms.
database_entries.reasoning: Direct match — LIC Market Plus maps to CMA Govt Securities Current (sheet_row 181, III_A10a).

database_entries.industry_note: All mutual funds (Baroda BNP Paribas, Edelweiss, Nippon, Parag Pareikh, TATA) and listed shares (Ceditaccess Grameen, JSW Infra, Tata Tech, Adani Wilmar, Aditya Birla, LIC shares) aggregated as 'Other current investments' (III_A10c). These are personal investments of the proprietor on the business balance sheet. In a Pvt Ltd company, these would require disclosure of relationship and purpose.
database_entries.reasoning: Direct match — aggregate of all mutual fund and share investments maps to CMA Other current investments (sheet_row 184, III_A10c). Total of 11 investment items = Rs 546,144.

database_entries.industry_note: Jewellery — a personal asset of the proprietor — classified as 'Other non current investments' (III_A10d). This is clearly a non-business asset but appears on the proprietorship balance sheet. The CA classified it as non-current because jewellery is not held for trading and has no expected maturity/redemption date. Amount unchanged across all three years (Rs 4.873 lakhs).
database_entries.reasoning: Direct match — Jewellery maps to CMA Other non-current investments (sheet_row 185, III_A10d). Same amount in FY22, FY23, FY24.

database_entries.raw_text: Stock-in-Trade ( Valued at Cost Or Market price Whichever Is Lower)
database_entries.industry_note: In trading firms, 'Stock-in-Trade' (finished goods held for resale) is classified as 'Stocks-in-process' (III_A13) rather than 'Finished Goods' (III_A14) in CMA Form III. This is standard CA practice for trading entities — the CMA template treats trading inventory as work-in-process. Note: Finished Goods row (III_A14) is zero. FY23 had an unusually high stock (Rs 16.65 lakhs) which may have been goods in transit.
database_entries.reasoning: Direct match — Stock-in-Trade closing balance maps to CMA Stocks-in-process (sheet_row 199, III_A13).

database_entries.cma_field_name: Domestic Receivables ( including bills purchased and discounted)
database_entries.reasoning: Direct match — Sundry Debtors total maps to CMA Domestic Receivables (sheet_row 205, III_A15a). No export receivables. Includes negative balances for some parties (Amrit Pharmacy -60,181, Tally -118) netted within total. Major debtor: Honeywell Automation (Rs 22.61 lakhs).
Direct match — Cash in Hand maps to CMA Cash on Hand (sheet_row 211, III_A16a).

database_entries.industry_note: FY24 Bank Balances include Kotak Mahindra Bank overdraft (-Rs 17,56,362) and Kotak Sweep Account (Rs 20,40,000). In FY22, the Kotak Sweep was classified separately as FD under lien (III_A16c). In FY23, it was classified as Other Fixed Deposits (III_A16d). In FY24, the CA lumped everything together in Bank Balances. This inconsistent treatment of the Sweep Account across years is a CA judgment call that affects the Cash & Bank breakdown.
database_entries.reasoning: Direct match — Total Bank Balance maps to CMA Bank Balances (sheet_row 212, III_A16b). Includes 6 bank accounts with mixed signs.
database_entries.validation_issue: FY24 includes Kotak overdraft (-17.56L) and Sweep account (20.40L) within Bank Balances. In prior years these were shown separately.

database_entries.raw_text: Kotak - Sweep Account (classified as FD under lien)
database_entries.industry_note: FY2021-22 only: Kotak Sweep Account (Rs 17,60,000) classified as 'Fixed Deposit under lien' (III_A16c). A sweep account automatically invests excess bank balance in short-term FDs linked to the bank account, hence the 'FD under lien' classification. In FY23, a larger amount went to 'Other Fixed Deposits' (III_A16d). In FY24, it was merged back into Bank Balances.
database_entries.reasoning: FY22 specific — Kotak Sweep Account split from Bank Balance into FD under lien.

database_entries.raw_text: Kotak - Sweep Account + Kotak FD (classified as Other FDs)
database_entries.industry_note: FY2022-23 only: Combined Kotak Sweep Account (Rs 40,10,000 from Bank Balances) + Kotak FD (Rs 7,02,607.20 from Investments) = Rs 47,12,607.20 placed in 'Other Fixed Deposits' (III_A16d). The CA aggregated two FD-like instruments from different BS sections into one CMA row. In FY22, only the Sweep went to FD under lien. In FY24, nothing is in this row. Large FD fluctuation across years.
database_entries.reasoning: FY23 specific — Kotak Sweep + Kotak FD investment combined into Other FDs. Sum: 4,010,000 + 702,607.20 = 4,712,607.20 = CMA 47.126072. VALIDATED ✓

database_entries.reasoning: Component 2 of 2 for CMA Advances recoverable (sheet_row 218, III_A17a). Input tax credit receivable. Sum: 44,586.86 + 56,590.97 = 101,177.83 ≈ CMA 1.01178 lakhs. VALIDATED ✓
Direct match — Advance Tax & TDS balance maps to CMA Advance Income Tax (sheet_row 220, III_A17c).

database_entries.reasoning: Direct match — Sundry Creditors for Purchases maps to CMA Sundry Creditors for goods (sheet_row 241, III_L10a). Large volatility: Rs 44.45L (FY22) → Rs 88.69L (FY23) → Rs 24.22L (FY24).

Component 2 of 2 for CMA Other statutory liabilities (sheet_row 245, III_L10e). TDS deducted but not yet deposited. Sum: 131,661.04 + 8,163 = 139,824.04 ≈ CMA 1.39824 lakhs. VALIDATED ✓
Direct match — Expense Payable maps to CMA Creditors for Expenses (sheet_row 248, III_L10h). Large increase from Rs 0.56L (FY23) to Rs 6.05L (FY24) — likely accrued salary/consultancy payable.

database_entries.reasoning: FY22 specific: Component of Advances recoverable. IT refund due from AY 2020-21. In FY22, Advances recoverable = IT Refund (14,420) + GST Receivable (56,590.97) = 71,010.97 = CMA 0.71011 lakhs. This item was present only in FY22 BS.
FY23 only: Addition of Mobile VIVO Y21 (Rs 13,688) — the only FA addition across all three years. CMA III_A6 = 0 in FY22 and FY24.

database_entries.raw_text: FD Accrued Interest (reclassified from Investments to Kotak FD)
database_entries.reasoning: FY23 specific: FD Accrued Interest (Rs 33,204.18) appears in Investments but mapped to Govt Securities row along with LIC. CMA III_A10a FY23 = 1.0691118 = LIC (0.73707) + FD Interest (0.332). VALIDATED ✓
database_entries.validation_issue: FY23 CMA III_A10a = 1.069 lakhs. LIC (73,707) + FD Accrued Interest (33,204.18) = 106,911.18 = 1.06911 lakhs ≈ CMA 1.0691118. Some FD interest may have been classified here instead of with Kotak FD (which went to III_A16d). Mapping uncertain — flagging as approximate.
